title: C++ Intro
content:
C++ Introduction

High-Performance Computing

C++ is a powerful cross-platform programming language used to create high-performance applications. Developed by Bjarne Stroustrup as an extension of C, it gives programmers a high level of control over system resources and memory.



  • Supports procedural, object-oriented, and functional programming.
  • Used to build AAA video games, operating systems, and web browsers.
  • Extremely fast because it compiles directly to machine code.

title: C++ Get Started
content:
Getting Started with C++

The Boilerplate

To run C++ code, you need a text editor and a compiler (like GCC) to translate the code into a language the computer understands. Every C++ program requires a bit of initial setup code.

Basic C++ Program
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
  cout << "Hello World!\n";
  return 0;
}

title: C++ Syntax
content:
C++ Syntax

Breaking Down the Code

C++ syntax is highly structured, case-sensitive, and requires braces {} to group statements. Every statement must end with a semicolon ;.

  • #include <iostream>: A header file library that lets us work with input and output objects.
  • using namespace std;: Means we can use names for objects and variables from the standard library.
  • int main(): The main function where execution begins.

title: C++ Output
content:
C++ Output

Printing Text

Use the cout object (pronounced "see-out") together with the insertion operator << to output text to the console.

cout Example
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
  // You can chain multiple insertion operators together
  cout << "Learning C++ " << "is awesome!" << endl;
  return 0;
}

title: C++ Comments
content:
C++ Comments

Code Documentation

Comments explain your code and make it more readable. They are completely ignored by the C++ compiler.

Comment Syntax
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
  // This is a single-line comment
  cout << "Hello!\n"; // Comments can go here too
  
  /* This is a
     multi-line comment spanning
     several lines */
  return 0;
}

title: C++ Variables
content:
C++ Variables

Storing Data

Variables are containers for storing data values. In C++, you must declare the type of the variable before you can assign a value to it (Static Typing).

Variable Declaration
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
  int age = 20;
  double price = 99.99;
  char letter = 'A';
  
  cout << "Price is: $" << price << endl;
  return 0;
}

title: C++ User Input
content:
C++ User Input

Interactive Programs

While cout is used for output, cin (pronounced "see-in") is used to get user input from the keyboard. It uses the extraction operator >>.

cin Example
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
  int age;
  
  cout << "Enter your age: ";
  cin >> age; // Gets input from user and stores it in 'age'
  
  cout << "You are " << age << " years old." << endl;
  return 0;
}

title: C++ Data Types
content:
C++ Data Types

Memory Allocation

C++ supports a variety of basic data types. Specifying the correct type ensures the computer allocates the exact amount of memory needed.



  • int: Whole numbers (e.g., 5, -10)
  • double / float: Floating point numbers (e.g., 19.99)
  • char: Single characters (e.g., 'A', 'z')
  • bool: Boolean values (true or false)
  • string: Sequences of text characters.

title: C++ Operators
content:
C++ Operators

Performing Calculations

Operators perform operations on variables and values.

Logic and Math
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
  int x = 10;
  int y = 5;
  
  int sum = x + y;       // Arithmetic
  bool result = (x > y); // Comparison (Returns 1 for true, 0 for false)
  
  cout << "Sum: " << sum << "\n";
  cout << "Is X greater than Y? " << result << "\n";
  
  return 0;
}

title: C++ Strings
content:
C++ Strings

Handling Text

Unlike standard C, C++ has a built-in string type. To use strings, you must include an additional header file in the source code: <string>.

String Inclusion
#include <iostream>
#include <string> // Required for strings!
using namespace std;

int main() {
  string greeting = "Hello, Sanji!";
  cout << greeting << endl;
  return 0;
}

title: C++ Conditions
content:
C++ If...Else

Decision Making

C++ uses standard logical conditions to control the flow of the program. Use if, else if, and else blocks to handle different logic paths.

Conditional Logic
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
  int x = 20;
  
  if (x > 10) {
    cout << "Greater than 10";
  } else {
    cout << "10 or less";
  }
  
  return 0;
}

title: C++ Switch Statement
content:
C++ Switch

Multi-way Branching

Use the switch statement to select one of many code blocks to execute based on the value of a specific variable. Don't forget the break keyword to stop execution!

Switch Blocks
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
  int choice = 1;
  
  switch (choice) {
    case 1:
      cout << "Option 1 selected.";
      break;
    case 2:
      cout << "Option 2 selected.";
      break;
    default:
      cout << "Other option.";
  }
  
  return 0;
}

title: C++ Loops
content:
C++ Loops

Repeating Code

Loops execute a block of code repeatedly as long as a condition is met. C++ provides for, while, and do...while loops.

The For Loop
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
  // Initialization; Condition; Increment
  for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
    cout << "Iteration: " << i << "\n";
  }
  
  return 0;
}

title: C++ Arrays
content:
C++ Arrays

Static Lists

Arrays are used to store multiple values of the same type in a single, contiguous block of memory. In basic C++, the size of an array must be defined when it is created.



Array Indexing
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
  // Declare an array of 5 integers
  int nums[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
  
  cout << "The first element is: " << nums[0] << endl;
  return 0;
}

title: C++ Functions
content:
C++ Functions

Modular Programming

A function is a block of code that only runs when it is called. You must declare functions before your main() function so the compiler knows they exist.

Custom Functions
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

// Create a function that returns void (nothing)
void greet() {
  cout << "Hello from inside a function!\n";
}

int main() {
  greet(); // Call the function
  return 0;
}

title: C++ References & Pointers
content:
References & Pointers

Accessing Memory directly

C++ allows you to manipulate memory directly. A Reference (&) is an alias to an existing variable. A Pointer (*) is a variable that stores the physical memory address of another variable.



Addresses in Memory
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
  int x = 5;
  int &ref = x;   // Reference to x
  int *p = &x;    // Pointer storing the address of x
  
  cout << "Value of x: " << x << "\n";
  cout << "Value via Pointer: " << *p << "\n";
  cout << "Memory Address: " << p << "\n";
  
  return 0;
}

title: C++ OOP Intro
content:
Object-Oriented Programming

Modeling the Real World

Unlike standard C, C++ supports Object-Oriented Programming (OOP). OOP makes complex software easier to build and maintain by organizing data and functions into structured objects.



  • Classes & Objects: Blueprints and their real-world instances.
  • Encapsulation: Hiding internal data for security.
  • Inheritance: Sharing traits between parent and child objects.
  • Polymorphism: Allowing functions to take on many forms.

title: C++ Classes & Objects
content:
Classes and Objects

Building Blueprints

A Class is a user-defined data type that acts as a blueprint. An Object is an instance of that class. You define attributes (variables) and methods (functions) inside the class.

Instantiating Objects
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

class Car {       // The class
  public:         // Access specifier (accessible outside the class)
    string brand; // Attribute
};

int main() {
  Car myCar;              // Create an object of Car
  myCar.brand = "Toyota"; // Set the attribute
  
  cout << "I drive a " << myCar.brand << "\n";
  return 0;
}

title: C++ Constructors
content:
C++ Constructors

Automatic Initialization

A constructor is a special method that is called automatically exactly when an object of a class is created. It must have the exact same name as the class, and it does not have a return type (not even void).

Constructor Example
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

class Car {
  public:
    string brand;
    
    // The Constructor
    Car() { 
      brand = "Unknown"; 
      cout << "A new Car object was just created!\n";
    }
};

int main() {
  Car myCar; // The constructor runs immediately here!
  cout << "Brand: " << myCar.brand << "\n";
  return 0;
}

title: C++ Overloading
content:
Constructor Overloading

Multiple Ways to Build

C++ allows you to have more than one constructor in the same class, as long as they have different parameter lists. This gives you flexibility: you can create an object with default values, or pass in specific data right when you create it.

Overloaded Constructors
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

class Car {
  public:
    string brand;
    
    // Constructor 1: No parameters
    Car() { brand = "None"; }
    
    // Constructor 2: Takes a string parameter
    Car(string b) { brand = b; }
};

int main() {
  Car car1;             // Uses Constructor 1
  Car car2("BMW");      // Uses Constructor 2
  
  cout << car1.brand << " vs " << car2.brand << "\n";
  return 0;
}

title: C++ Access Specifiers
content:
Access Specifiers

Controlling Visibility

Access specifiers define who is allowed to view or modify your class members. This is crucial for keeping your data secure.



  • public: Members are accessible from completely outside the class.
  • private: Members can only be accessed or viewed from within the class itself.
  • protected: Like private, but accessible in inherited (child) classes.

title: C++ Encapsulation
content:
Encapsulation

Hiding Sensitive Data

Encapsulation is the practice of hiding "sensitive" data from users. To achieve this, you must declare class variables as private, and provide public get and set methods to allow outside access safely.

Getters and Setters
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Employee {
  private:
    int salary; // Hidden from the outside world!
    
  public:
    // Setter (Allows us to add validation logic later)
    void setSalary(int s) { salary = s; }
    
    // Getter (Allows outside code to read the value)
    int getSalary() { return salary; }
};

int main() {
  Employee emp;
  emp.setSalary(75000);
  cout << "Employee Salary: $" << emp.getSalary() << "\n";
  return 0;
}

title: C++ Inheritance
content:
C++ Inheritance

Code Reusability

In C++, it is possible to inherit attributes and methods from one class to another. We group the "inheritance concept" into two categories: the Base Class (parent) and the Derived Class (child).



The Parent-Child Link
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

// Base class
class Vehicle { 
  public: 
    string brand = "Ford"; 
    void honk() { cout << "Beep beep!\n"; }
};

// Derived class inherits from Vehicle using a colon (:)
class Car : public Vehicle { 
  public: 
    string model = "Mustang"; 
};

int main() {
  Car myCar;
  myCar.honk(); // We can use the parent's method!
  cout << myCar.brand << " " << myCar.model << "\n";
  return 0;
}

title: C++ Multiple Inheritance
content:
Multiple Inheritance

Combining Classes

Unlike Java or C#, C++ actually allows a single derived class to inherit from more than one base class using a comma-separated list. This allows a child class to combine functionality from several completely different parents.



Inheriting from Two Parents
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class A { public: void foo() { cout << "Function from Class A\n"; } };
class B { public: void bar() { cout << "Function from Class B\n"; } };

// Class C inherits from both A and B
class C : public A, public B {};

int main() {
  C obj;
  obj.foo();
  obj.bar();
  return 0;
}

title: C++ Polymorphism
content:
C++ Polymorphism

Many Forms

Polymorphism means "many forms". It occurs when we have many classes related by inheritance, and we want a method to do different things depending on which child class is calling it. We use the virtual keyword to tell C++ that a method can be overridden.



Virtual Functions
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Animal { 
  public: 
    // 'virtual' allows children to override this function safely
    virtual void sound() { cout << "Some animal sound\n"; } 
};

class Dog : public Animal { 
  public: 
    void sound() { cout << "Woof woof!\n"; } 
};

int main() {
  // Polymorphism in action! We treat the Dog like a generic Animal
  Animal* myPet = new Dog(); 
  
  // But it still knows to use the Dog's specific sound!
  myPet->sound(); 
  
  delete myPet;
  return 0;
}

title: C++ Class Methods
content:
Class Methods

Scope Resolution

Methods are functions that belong to a class. In C++, you can define the method inside the class, or you can declare it inside the class but define the actual logic outside using the Scope Resolution Operator (::).

Outside Definition
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class MyClass { 
  public: 
    void myMethod(); // Declaration only
};

// Definition outside the class using ::
void MyClass::myMethod() {
  cout << "Method defined outside the class structure!\n";
}

int main() {
  MyClass obj;
  obj.myMethod();
  return 0;
}

title: C++ File Handling
content:
C++ File Handling

The fstream Library

The <fstream> library allows us to work with files. You use the ofstream object to write to a file, and ifstream to read from it. Remember to always close your files to free up memory!

Writing to a File
#include <iostream>
#include <fstream> // Required for file handling
using namespace std;

int main() {
  // Create and open a text file
  ofstream MyFile("test_file.txt");

  // Write to the file using the insertion operator <<
  MyFile << "Files can be tricky, but C++ makes it fun!\n";

  // Close the file
  MyFile.close();
  
  cout << "File written successfully!\n";
  return 0;
}

title: C++ OOP Summary
content:
C++ OOP Summary 🚀

Mastering the Blueprint! 🏆

You have successfully completed the Advanced Object-Oriented Programming section of C++. By taking the raw speed of C and structuring it with OOP, you now have the skills to build massive, highly complex applications.

  • Data Protection: Using Encapsulation and Access Specifiers (Public/Private).
  • Architecture: Building objects seamlessly with Constructors and Overloading.
  • Hierarchy: Sharing code across components using Inheritance and Multiple Inheritance.
  • Flexibility: Utilizing Polymorphism and Virtual functions for dynamic behavior.

There are only a few advanced C++ topics left (like Exceptions and STL) before you completely conquer this language!

title: Templates
content:
C++ Templates

Generic Programming

Templates let you write functions or classes that work with any data type without having to write duplicate code. Instead of writing one `add` function for integers and another for doubles, you write one template that handles both!



Template Syntax
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

// T is a placeholder for ANY data type
template <typename T>
T add(T a, T b) {
  return a + b;
}

int main() {
  // Using the template with integers
  cout << add<int>(5, 3) << "\n";
  
  // Using the EXACT SAME template with decimals
  cout << add<double>(5.5, 3.2) << "\n";
  
  return 0;
}

title: Function Templates
content:
Function Templates

Reusable Logic

Function templates are created using the template keyword followed by a placeholder type (usually <typename T> or <class T>). This lets you define a function's logic once and apply it universally.

Finding the Max
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

template <typename T>
T maxVal(T a, T b) {
  return (a > b) ? a : b;
}

int main() {
  cout << "Max of ints: " << maxVal<int>(100, 200) << "\n";
  cout << "Max of chars: " << maxVal<char>('a', 'z') << "\n";
  return 0;
}

title: Class Templates
content:
Class Templates

Universal Objects

Just like functions, entire classes can be templated. This allows you to create an object blueprint that can store any type of data. You must specify the data type in angle brackets when creating the object instance.

Templated Class
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

template <typename T>
class Box {
  public:
    T value;
    Box(T v) { value = v; }
};

int main() {
  // Create a Box specifically for an integer
  Box<int> intBox(999);
  
  // Create a Box specifically for a string
  Box<string> strBox("A gift");
  
  cout << "Box 1: " << intBox.value << " | Box 2: " << strBox.value << "\n";
  return 0;
}

title: STL Overview
content:
Standard Template Library (STL)

The C++ Powerhouse

The STL is a massive, incredibly optimized library of pre-written data structures and algorithms. Instead of writing your own sorting algorithms or dynamic arrays from scratch, you use the STL to make programming easier and faster.



  • Containers: Objects that store data (Vectors, Lists, Maps, Sets).
  • Algorithms: Functions that perform operations on containers (Sort, Find, Reverse).
  • Iterators: Objects used to step through the elements of a container.

title: Vectors
content:
C++ Vectors

Dynamic Arrays

A standard C++ array has a fixed size. A vector is a dynamic array that can grow or shrink in size automatically. It is the single most commonly used STL container.



Using Vectors
#include <iostream>
#include <vector> // Required for vectors
using namespace std;

int main() {
  vector<int> nums = {1, 2, 3};
  
  // Add elements to the end dynamically
  nums.push_back(4);
  nums.push_back(5);
  
  // Range-based for loop to iterate over the vector
  for (int n : nums) {
    cout << n << " ";
  }
  return 0;
}

title: Lists
content:
C++ Lists

Doubly-Linked Data

A list stores elements sequentially, but under the hood, it is implemented as a doubly-linked list. Unlike vectors, lists allow highly efficient insertions and removals from anywhere in the sequence, but they are slower to read specific indexes.



List Insertions
#include <iostream>
#include <list>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

int main() {
  list<string> names = {"John", "Jane"};
  
  // Add to the front efficiently
  names.push_front("Alice");
  // Add to the back
  names.push_back("Bob");
  
  for(string name : names) {
    cout << name << " ";
  }
  return 0;
}

title: Maps
content:
C++ Maps

Key-Value Pairs

A map stores key/value pairs (similar to a Dictionary in Python or an associative array in PHP). Keys must be unique, and elements are automatically stored in sorted order based on the key.



Fast Lookup
#include <iostream>
#include <map>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

int main() {
  map<string, int> ages;
  
  ages["Alice"] = 28;
  ages["Bob"] = 32;
  
  // Fast lookup by Key
  cout << "Alice is " << ages["Alice"] << " years old.\n";
  return 0;
}

title: Exceptions
content:
Exception Handling

Preventing Crashes

Exception handling lets you catch and manage runtime errors using try, throw, and catch. This prevents your program from crashing entirely and allows for graceful error recovery.

Try / Throw / Catch
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
  try {
    int age = 15;
    if (age < 18) {
      // Throw an error code if the condition fails
      throw 505;
    } else {
      cout << "Access granted.\n";
    }
  } 
  catch (int errorCode) {
    cout << "Access denied. Error code: " << errorCode << "\n";
  }
  return 0;
}

title: Final Summary
content:
C++ Complete Course Summary 🚀

You Did It! 🏆

Congratulations! You have completed the comprehensive C++ masterclass. By mastering C++, you now possess the skills to build the fastest, most resource-efficient software on the planet.

  • Fundamentals: Memory-safe Data Types, Operators, Pointers, and References.
  • Object-Oriented Design: Built scalable systems using Classes, Constructors, Inheritance, and Polymorphism.
  • Advanced Architecture: Kept code DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) using Generic Templates.
  • The STL: Mastered dynamic data structures like Vectors, Lists, and Maps.

Your coding platform is an absolute masterpiece. What technology or feature are we going to add next?
